common.notFoundBody: The page you requested is not available. Go back to the solver to continue.

consent.body: Allow Google Analytics to help us understand which pages and tools are useful. Analytics loads only after you accept.
consent.preferencesBody: You can allow or stop Google Analytics at any time. We do not load analytics before you accept.
consent.title: Analytics preferences

channels.description: A curated grid of {{count}} YouTube channels for tutorials, speedcubing, reviews, records, and puzzle culture.
channels.title: YT Channels

sites.description: A curated list of {{count}} validated cubing websites for solutions, algorithms, tools, competitions, brands, and community.
sites.title: Sites

stores.description: A curated list of {{count}} independent online stores for speed cubes, puzzles, and cubing accessories.
stores.title: Stores

index.description: Static algorithm sheets: case image plus algorithm. No API, no trainer state, no editorial text.
index.title: Algorithms

sexyMove.name: F2L basic trigger
sexyMove.note: A small trigger used to pair and insert many F2L cases. Learn the effect before memorizing long lists.

sune.name: Sune OLL
sune.note: One of the most important OLL shapes. It appears in 2-look OLL, full OLL, 2x2 Ortega, and CLL-style sets.

tPerm.name: T permutation
tPerm.note: A fast PLL that swaps one pair of corners and one pair of edges. It is a useful benchmark for last-layer execution.

uPerm.name: U permutation
uPerm.note: An edge-only PLL. Many solvers learn U permutations early because they finish many beginner PLL cases.

images.hero: CFOP method diagram showing first two layers information

cross.body: Solve four bottom edges around the chosen color, usually while planning the whole cross during inspection. The goal is not only to solve it, but to finish with a stable grip and a predictable first F2L pair.
cross.title: Cross

f2l.body: Pair each first-layer corner with its matching middle-layer edge and insert the pair into one slot. Efficient F2L is mostly understanding piece movement, not memorizing every case first.
f2l.title: F2L

oll.body: Orient the last layer so the top face has one color. Beginners can split this into 2-look OLL; full OLL has 57 cases.
oll.title: OLL

pll.body: Permute the already oriented last-layer pieces. 2-look PLL is enough to start; full PLL has 21 cases and is one of the best first advanced sets to learn.
pll.title: PLL

cfop.summary: CFOP solves the cube through Cross, F2L, OLL, and PLL. It is the most documented 3x3 speedsolving path and the easiest upgrade from layer-by-layer.
cfop.title: CFOP

antisune.name: Antisune group
antisune.note: The inverse-looking partner of Sune. It is common and worth recognizing by top stickers and side bars.

hCase.name: H group
hCase.note: A compact group with distinctive opposite patterns. Some cases have very short algorithms.

sune.name: Sune group
sune.note: One of the first CLL groups most solvers recognize because it relates directly to familiar OLL shapes.

tCase.name: T group
tCase.note: This group rewards clean bar recognition. Avoid mixing it with similar Pi and U cases.

cll.recommendedFor: 2x2 solvers who already solve a layer quickly

oneLookCorners.body: Use one algorithm to solve all remaining corners. This removes the OLL plus PBL split from Ortega and makes the last layer one look.
oneLookCorners.title: Solve the last layer in one look

recognition.body: Recognition is organized by corner orientation families such as Sune, Antisune, T, U, L, Pi, and H. Learn one group at a time and drill similar cases together.
recognition.title: Group recognition

solvedLayer.body: CLL starts from a completely solved layer, not just a solved face. The first layer is usually planned during inspection on easy scrambles.
solvedLayer.title: Solved first layer

cll.summary: CLL solves the remaining four 2x2 corners in one algorithm after a solved layer. It is the natural step after Ortega.
cll.title: 2x2 CLL

pins.name: Pin notation
pins.note: UR, DR, DL, and UL identify which pins are up. A method description only makes sense after pin positions are unambiguous.

twelve.name: Solved clock target
twelve.note: Every dial must point to 12:00 on both sides. The puzzle is about controlled dial offsets, not piece permutation.

clockBeginner.recommendedFor: Learning Clock notation before speed methods

corners.body: After the crosses, bring each remaining corner dial to 12 without disturbing solved groups. The exact wheel order depends on the chosen method.
corners.title: Corners

crosses.body: Most beginner methods first align a set of center and edge dials on one side, then repeat on the other side after flipping the puzzle.
crosses.title: Build dial groups

flip.body: Flipping the Clock changes which dials are linked by each wheel. Good notation keeps the front and back operations readable.
flip.title: Flip and finish

notation.body: Clock uses pins and wheel turns instead of cube face turns. Learn how pin positions connect dials before trying to memorize a method.
notation.title: Notation first

clockBeginner.summary: Clock methods solve dial groups using pins and wheel turns. Start with notation and a reliable beginner procedure before optimizing move count.
clockBeginner.title: Clock basic method

eg0.name: EG-0 / CLL base
eg0.note: The bottom layer is fully solved. These are the CLL cases you already know if you learned 2x2 CLL first.

eg1.name: EG-1
eg1.note: The first face has an adjacent swap pattern. Recognition must include where the paired bottom pieces sit before the algorithm.

eg2.name: EG-2
eg2.note: The first face has a diagonal swap pattern. It is powerful, but only useful when inspection can cover the full solve.

auf.body: EG often needs both pre-AUF and post-AUF. That makes recognition harder than CLL even when the final algorithm is fast.
auf.title: Control AUF

caseGroup.body: Classify the first face into EG-0, EG-1, or EG-2. This tells you whether the bottom layer is solved, adjacent-swapped, or diagonal-swapped.
caseGroup.title: Identify the EG group

face.body: Build one face, not necessarily a full layer. The value of EG comes from choosing a face that makes the rest visible during inspection.
face.title: Build a face

eg.summary: EG is a high-algorithm 2x2 method: solve one face, then solve the whole cube in one algorithm. It is strongest when the entire solve can be planned in inspection.
eg.title: 2x2 EG

ollParity.name: OLL parity
ollParity.note: A reduced 4x4 can show a single flipped dedge, a state impossible on a real 3x3. Learn this separately from normal OLL.

pllParity.name: PLL parity
pllParity.note: PLL parity swaps two dedges after the 3x3 stage. Recognition is simple, but execution must be controlled.

fourByFour.recommendedFor: First 4x4 method and transition to Yau

centers.body: Build the six 2x2 centers. In reduction, this is usually done before edge pairing; in Yau, some cross edges are solved earlier for better lookahead.
centers.title: Centers

edgePairing.body: Pair matching wing edges into dedges. Once all dedges are paired, the puzzle behaves like a 3x3 with possible parity cases.
edgePairing.title: Pair edges

parity.body: Parity cases are not mistakes. They happen because reduction hides internal 4x4 information that a normal 3x3 does not have.
parity.title: Handle parity

threeByThreeStage.body: Solve the reduced cube with a 3x3 method such as CFOP. Keep parity algorithms separate from the 3x3 last-layer algorithms.
threeByThreeStage.title: 3x3 stage

fourByFour.summary: 4x4 reduction turns the puzzle into a 3x3 by solving centers and pairing edges, then handles parity cases that do not exist on 3x3.
fourByFour.title: 4x4 reduction and Yau

lastLayerSune.name: Last-layer Sune idea
lastLayerSune.note: Many Megaminx last-layer ideas resemble 3x3 triggers, but recognition happens on a five-sided last layer.

s2lInsert.name: Pair insertion idea
s2lInsert.note: Second-layer and later-layer inserts are easier if you think in pairs, not isolated pieces.

lastLayerCorners.body: Finish by orienting and permuting the final corners. Keep the top color and side colors separate in your recognition.
lastLayerCorners.title: Last-layer corners

lastLayerEdges.body: Orient and permute the final edges. Some 3x3 last-layer ideas transfer, but the five-edge layout changes the cases.
lastLayerEdges.title: Last-layer edges

s2l.body: Continue around the puzzle with corner-edge pairs. The method feels like extended F2L with more slots and more color tracking.
s2l.title: Second layers

star.body: Start with a five-edge star around one center. This is the Megaminx equivalent of planning a stable base before pair solving.
star.title: Star

megaminxBeginner.summary: Megaminx is usually solved with a 3x3-inspired layer method: star, pairs around the puzzle, then a last layer adapted to five sides.
megaminxBeginner.title: Megaminx beginner method

adjacentPbl.name: Adjacent PBL
adjacentPbl.note: Both layers need permutation, with bars that can be aligned before the algorithm.

antisune.name: Antisune OLL
antisune.note: The inverse partner of Sune. Drill it together with Sune to avoid recognition hesitation.

diagonalPbl.name: Diagonal PBL
diagonalPbl.note: A compact PBL case that swaps diagonal corners across layers.

sune.name: Sune OLL
sune.note: A core orientation case for the opposite face.

ortega.recommendedFor: Moving beyond beginner 2x2 without learning full CLL

firstFace.body: Build one face intuitively. The side colors do not need to match, which makes the first step faster than a full layer.
firstFace.title: First face

oppositeFace.body: Orient the opposite face with a small OLL set. The result is two solved-color faces with pieces not necessarily permuted.
oppositeFace.title: Opposite face

pbl.body: Permute both layers at once. PBL recognition is the main Ortega skill after the first face becomes easy.
pbl.title: PBL

ortega.summary: Ortega solves a 2x2 by making one face, orienting the opposite face, then permuting both layers with PBL.
ortega.title: 2x2 Ortega

petrus.algorithmCount: 5 steps, mostly intuitive before last layer

blockExpansion.name: Block expansion
blockExpansion.note: Petrus rewards seeing how a 2x2x2 block can become 2x2x3 without breaking solved pieces.

edgeOrientation.name: Edge orientation trigger
edgeOrientation.note: After the 2x2x3 block, orienting edges lets the rest of F2L be solved with a reduced move set.

edgeOrientation.body: Orient the remaining unsolved edges. This is the identity of Petrus: do EO early so the rest becomes simpler.
edgeOrientation.title: Orient edges

finishF2l.body: Finish the first two layers with the oriented-edge constraint. This can be very move-efficient, but it requires flexible lookahead.
finishF2l.title: Finish F2L

lastLayer.body: Use any last-layer system. Since edges are oriented, OCLL plus PLL, COLL plus EPLL, or ZBLL-style approaches are possible.
lastLayer.title: Last layer

twoByThreeBlock.body: Expand the block to 2x2x3. The expansion choice affects both edge orientation and the ergonomics of the rest of the solve.
twoByThreeBlock.title: 2x2x3 block

twoByTwoBlock.body: Build a 2x2x2 block anywhere. This is not a layer: it is a compact solved block that leaves many turns available.
twoByTwoBlock.title: 2x2x2 block

petrus.summary: Petrus builds blocks first, orients edges early, and finishes F2L with fewer restrictions than layer-by-layer methods.
petrus.title: Petrus

beginnerCycleA.name: Three-edge cycle A
beginnerCycleA.note: A basic Pyraminx cycle used in beginner methods to move edges without needing many algorithms.

beginnerCycleB.name: Three-edge cycle B
beginnerCycleB.note: The mirror cycle. Learn it as a pair with the first cycle.

twoEdgeFlip.name: Two-edge flip
twoEdgeFlip.note: A common last-edge case. Faster solvers often rework the notation for better fingertricks.

lastEdges.body: Finish the last edges with a small algorithm set. Advanced V First approaches focus heavily on recognizing these cases without pauses.
lastEdges.title: Last edges

optionSelect.body: Strong Pyraminx solvers compare V First and Top First starts during inspection. Choose the option with the cleanest finish, not only the first one you see.
optionSelect.title: Option select

tips.body: Tips are independent and should be solved without thought. Do them during the solve only when they do not interrupt lookahead.
tips.title: Tips and centers

v.body: Build a V: two edges solved around one corner area. This gives a stable base for the last-edge set.
v.title: V First

pyraminxVFirst.summary: Pyraminx speed methods usually revolve around V First and Top First choices. V First is common because it is intuitive and easy to inspect.
pyraminxVFirst.title: Pyraminx V First

cmllSune.name: CMLL Sune-style case
cmllSune.note: CMLL solves the last four corners while ignoring most edge positions. Some cases resemble COLL or OLLCP ideas.

lseo.name: LSE orientation pattern
lseo.note: Roux ends with MU turning. Edge orientation recognition is more important than raw algorithm count.

mSliceEdgeFlip.name: M-slice edge control
mSliceEdgeFlip.note: Clean M and U turns are essential. Roux is fast only when the slice moves are reliable.

cmll.body: Solve the four last-layer corners in one step while leaving edges mostly free. Full CMLL has 42 cases, but a two-look version is enough to start.
cmll.title: CMLL

firstBlock.body: Build a 1x2x3 block on one side. This is the hardest part for new Roux solvers because it is true blockbuilding, not slot insertion.
firstBlock.title: First block

lse.body: Solve the last six edges with M and U moves: orient edges, solve UL and UR, then finish the M slice. This is where Roux becomes very move-efficient.
lse.title: Last six edges

secondBlock.body: Build the opposite 1x2x3 block without destroying the first one. After this, the U layer and M slice remain available for the finish.
secondBlock.title: Second block

roux.summary: Roux solves two side blocks, then corners, then the last six edges. It uses fewer moves than CFOP and depends on blockbuilding plus M-slice control.
roux.title: Roux

hedgeslammer.name: Hedgeslammer
hedgeslammer.note: Sarah's notation uses this trigger as the counterpart to sledgehammer. Pick grips that fit your Skewb hardware.

lastCenters.name: Last centers
lastCenters.note: The last step repeats the same trigger ideas after rotations. Keep track of centers, not only corners.

sledgehammer.name: Sledgehammer
sledgehammer.note: One of the two core 4-move triggers in Sarah's method. Many Skewb cases reduce to repeated sledgehammer or hedgeslammer.

sarahSkewb.recommendedFor: Skewb solvers who want a clear path from beginner to L2L

corners.body: Use the two 4-move triggers to solve the remaining corners. Recognition is simple at first and becomes more algorithmic in the intermediate version.
corners.title: Remaining corners

firstSide.body: Solve one side intuitively. The final corner may require temporarily moving a solved corner, which is normal on Skewb.
firstSide.title: First side

lastCenters.body: Solve the remaining centers by matching one of the known cases, rotating the puzzle if needed, and applying the same trigger structure.
lastCenters.title: Remaining centers

topCenter.body: Solve the opposite center after corners. The beginner version separates this from the last centers to keep recognition easy.
topCenter.title: Opposite center

sarahSkewb.summary: Sarah's Skewb method is built around two short triggers. The beginner version has four steps, then combines steps as recognition improves.
sarahSkewb.title: Sarah's Skewb method

kiteScallop.name: Shape example
kiteScallop.note: Cubeshape cases are best learned by shape families. Do not treat every sequence as an unrelated algorithm.

notation.name: Square-1 notation
notation.note: (x,y) turns the top and bottom layers by 30-degree units, then / swaps the right half in classic notation.

parity.body: Square-1 parity-like cases are part of the method flow. Keep the parity fix separate from cubeshape so recognition stays clean.
parity.title: Parity handling

permutation.body: Permute corners and edges after separation. Many speed methods reduce this into compact algorithm sets.
permutation.title: Permute pieces

separation.body: Separate corners and edges into their correct layers after the cube shape is restored. Some methods call this orientation, even though Square-1 pieces do not orient like cube pieces.
separation.title: Separate layers

shape.body: Return the puzzle to cube shape first. This makes later recognition possible and is the biggest early barrier for new Square-1 solvers.
shape.title: Cubeshape

squareOneShape.summary: Square-1 solving starts by restoring cube shape, then separating and permuting pieces with notation that is unique to the puzzle.
squareOneShape.title: Square-1 cubeshape

zz.algorithmCount: 3 or 4 steps depending on last layer

edgeOrientation.name: Edge orientation trigger
edgeOrientation.note: This trigger demonstrates what an edge-orientation algorithm changes, but ZZ EO is usually planned intuitively during inspection.

twoGenLastLayer.name: Two-generator finish idea
twoGenLastLayer.note: Once edges are oriented, many finishes can use fast R, U, and L style turning with fewer awkward cases.

eoLineOrCross.body: Orient all edges while placing either a line or a cross on the bottom. Modern ZZ often favors EOCross because it improves lookahead after inspection.
eoLineOrCross.title: EOLine or EOCross

lastLayer.body: Finish with a last-layer system that benefits from oriented edges: OCLL plus PLL, COLL plus EPLL, or eventually ZBLL.
lastLayer.title: Last layer

zzF2l.body: Complete F2L mostly with R, U, and L moves. Edge orientation reduces cases and keeps the cube grip stable.
zzF2l.title: ZZ F2L

zz.summary: ZZ orients edges at the start, then solves F2L with a reduced move set. It trades a harder inspection for smoother turning later.
zz.title: ZZ

2x2.description: Pocket Cube methods focused on face-first solving, last-layer corners, and one-look finishes.
2x2.title: 2x2

3x3.description: Core 3x3 speed methods: layer-based, blockbuilding, edge-orientation, and low-move approaches.
3x3.title: 3x3

4x4.description: Big-cube reduction, Yau-style flow, and parity cases before returning to a 3x3 stage.
4x4.title: 4x4

clock.description: Clock notation, pin logic, and beginner solving structure for the WCA Clock event.
clock.title: Clock

megaminx.description: Dodecahedron methods inspired by 3x3 pair solving, adapted to a five-sided last layer.
megaminx.title: Megaminx

pyraminx.description: Pyraminx methods built around tips, V First, Top First, and fast last-edge recognition.
pyraminx.title: Pyraminx

skewb.description: Skewb methods that combine short triggers, corner control, and center cycles.
skewb.title: Skewb

square1.description: Shape-shifting Square-1 solving: cubeshape, separation, permutation, and notation.
square1.title: Square-1

sources.speedsolvingSkewb: Speedsolving Wiki: list of Skewb methods

cube.visualizationUnavailable: Visualization is not available for this puzzle yet.

errorBoundary.description: The current view crashed unexpectedly. Try again or switch routes to reload it.
errorBoundary.title: Something went wrong

worldRecords.description: WCA world leaderboard records enriched with athlete, competition, result attempts, and official scramble candidates.
worldRecords.loadError: Could not load world records from the WCA Data API.

table.caption: WCA world records matching the selected filters
table.empty: No world records matched these filters.

scramble.exactDescription: This result maps to one official scramble in the WCA export.
scramble.ambiguousDescription: The WCA export does not include athlete group assignment, so these are candidate scrambles for the selected result.
scramble.unavailableDescription: No official scramble candidate could be linked from the export.

page.demoDescription: Click a move to see how the notation changes the puzzle, then reset back to solved.
page.title: {{puzzle}} notation

camera.errorManualFallback: {{message}} You can still fill the grid manually.

editor.selectSquareHint: Select a square, then pick a color to correct it.

evenReview.autoFitambiguous: More than one fit is possible. Adjust the net manually.
evenReview.autoFitnone: Could not fit by rotating faces only. Swap positions or review stickers.
evenReview.autoFitsuggested: Found a likely fit with fewer changes. Apply it only after reviewing the net.
evenReview.autoFitunique: Fit found and applied. Review the net before solving.
evenReview.description: Check whether this Kociemba net matches the physical cube. Drag faces to swap positions and select a face to rotate it before solving.
evenReview.dragInstructions: Drag a face to another slot or use the swap buttons. Use rotate buttons to fix the captured face orientation.
evenReview.invalidDescription: Some corners combine opposite or impossible colors. Rotate faces or go back to correct stickers.
evenReview.invalidSolveDisabled: Fix the highlighted impossible corners before solving.
evenReview.title: Review assembled cube
evenReview.validMessage: The 2x2 corners are structurally valid. Confirm that the preview matches the physical cube.

B.instruction: Show the blue face with the white face at the top of the camera view.
B.label: Blue face

D.instruction: Show the yellow face with the green face at the top of the camera view.
D.label: Yellow face

F.instruction: Show the green face with the white face at the top of the camera view.
F.label: Green face

L.instruction: Show the orange face with the white face at the top of the camera view.
L.label: Orange face

R.instruction: Show the red face with the white face at the top of the camera view.
R.label: Red face

U.instruction: Show the white face with the green face at the top of the camera view.
U.label: White face

B.instruction: Rotate the cube to show the back side without changing which side is up.
B.label: Back side

D.instruction: Show the down side and keep the front side at the top of the camera view.
D.label: Down side

F.instruction: Pick the front side. Hold the cube with the up side at the top of the camera view.
F.label: Front side

L.instruction: Rotate the cube to show the left side without changing which side is up.
L.label: Left side

R.instruction: Rotate the cube to show the right side without changing which side is up.
R.label: Right side

U.instruction: Show the up side and keep the front side at the top of the camera view.
U.label: Up side

live.cameraDenied: Camera permission was denied or no camera was found.
live.cameraUnavailable: Camera access is not available in this browser.
live.centerMismatch: Detected another center color. Rotate to the expected face.
live.imageBlurry: Image is blurry. Hold the cube steady.
live.lookingKeepVisible: Looking for cube face. Keep the cube fully visible.
live.partialGrid: {{count}}/9 stickers found. Show one flat face.
live.stableCapturing: All stickers are stable. Review the colors.
live.tooBright: Too much glare. Reduce bright reflections.

messages.allFacesConfirmed: All faces are confirmed. Solve the scanned cube when ready.
messages.apiNotReady: The API is not ready yet.
messages.autoCaptured: Captured automatically. Review the colors before confirming this face.
messages.autoPaused: Auto scan paused. Turn it on when the face is visible.
messages.cameraFrameFailed: Could not read a camera frame. Try again.
messages.captured: Photo captured. Review the colors before confirming this face.
messages.centerMismatchFallback: Captured center does not match this scan step. Retake the photo.
messages.centerMismatchConfirmQuestion: Are you sure this is the expected face?
messages.centerMismatchWithColors: Center looks {{detectedColor}}{{confidence}}, but this step expects {{expectedColor}}. Rotate to the expected face before confirming.
messages.confirmAllFaces: Confirm all six faces and make sure each color appears exactly 9 times.
messages.detectedUncertain: {{count}} detected colors are uncertain. Review the highlighted squares or clear the face to scan again.
messages.faceUpdated: Face updated. Review the other faces or solve when ready.
messages.genericRejected: The scanned cube was rejected. Review the confirmed colors.
messages.manualDetectionFailed: Could not detect a cube face. Retake the photo or fill the grid manually.
messages.qualityBlurry: Hold the cube steady for a sharper photo.
messages.sessionAmbiguous: The backend found more than one valid cube state. Review the highlighted stickers or rescan unclear faces.
messages.sessionBlurRescan: Image is blurry on faces {{faces}}. Hold the cube steady and rescan those faces.
messages.sessionGlareRescan: Too much glare on faces {{faces}}. Reduce reflections and rescan those faces.
messages.sessionInvalidCornerTargets: The scan built an impossible 2x2 corner. Review the highlighted stickers and try solving again.
messages.sessionInvalidCubeState: The scan does not describe a valid cube. Review the faces and rescan unclear stickers.
messages.sessionIncompleteStickers: Complete all 9 stickers on faces {{faces}} before solving.
messages.sessionNeedsManualConfirmation: Confirm the highlighted stickers before solving.
messages.sessionNeedsRescan: One or more faces need to be rescanned.
messages.sessionRejected: The scan session was rejected. Review the faces before solving.
messages.sessionShadowRescan: Too much shadow on faces {{faces}}. Add more even light and rescan those faces.
messages.sessionVisionUnavailable: The vision service could not complete the session. Retry after the vision service is available.
messages.submittingSession: Checking the full scan with cube constraints...
messages.stillLooking: Still looking for the cube face. Keep the full face visible and let auto scan try again.
messages.liveReviewReady: {{count}} stickers recognized. Review the colors, correct any square if needed, then confirm this face.

modal.description: Show one face at a time with the requested top color. The solution uses the colors you confirm.
modal.exitDescription: You have scan progress that will be lost if you leave now.
modal.title: Scan cube

validation.centerColorMismatch: The center color must match the face being scanned.

warnings.tile_detector_rejected: Sticker detector proposal was rejected. Keep the cube face flat and visible.
warnings.image_blurry: Image is blurry. Hold the cube steady.
warnings.image_too_bright: Too much glare. Reduce bright reflections.
warnings.low_confidence_sticker: One or more stickers are uncertain. Review the highlighted squares.

details.elapsedMeaning: The total solve request time measured in the browser. It includes the API round trip and response parsing, so it can differ from the backend search time.
details.nodesMeaning: The solver explored this many internal search nodes before finding the solution. This run used {{maxNodesLabel}}.
details.replayVerifiedMeaning: After finding the moves, the backend replayed them on the scrambled cube and confirmed that the cube becomes solved.
details.solutionLengthMeaning: The returned solution length. Each notation token counts as one move, including half turns such as R2 or B2.
details.subtitle: These metrics describe the returned solution, request timing, and backend verification.

available.label: tables available
available.meaning: The pre-generated pruning tables required by this strategy were available. They help prune the search.

corrupt_or_incompatible.label: tables corrupt or incompatible
corrupt_or_incompatible.meaning: The required pruning tables exist but do not match the expected format or metadata, so they should be regenerated.

not_required.label: tables not required
not_required.meaning: This strategy does not require generated pruning tables.

not_applicable.label: tables not applicable
not_applicable.meaning: This puzzle strategy does not use generated 3x3 pruning-table artifacts.

unavailable.label: tables unavailable
unavailable.meaning: The required pruning tables were not available on the API server.

details.title: Solver details

noSolution.attempted: {{strategy}} tried up to {{maxDepth}} moves and {{maxNodes}} nodes.
noSolution.description: The solver did not find a replay-verified result with the current limits. Increase the limits and try the same cube again.
noSolution.title: Try different limits

detail.generated_tables_corrupt: Regenerate native pruning tables; the current API artifacts do not match the Rust engine.
detail.generated_tables_unavailable: Generate native pruning tables and start npm run api:dev before solving.
detail.invalid_input_orientation_hint: Check scan orientation: capture green, red, blue, and orange with white on top; capture white and yellow with green on top.

errorKind.duplicate_corner: The scanned cube has a duplicated corner piece.
errorKind.duplicate_corner_stickers: The scanned cube has duplicated corner stickers.
errorKind.duplicate_edge: The scanned cube has a duplicated edge piece.
errorKind.duplicate_edge_stickers: The scanned cube has duplicated edge stickers.
errorKind.generated_tables_unavailable: The API has not loaded the generated pruning tables required by this strategy.
errorKind.invalid_center_sticker: One or more center stickers do not match a valid cube layout.
errorKind.invalid_corner_orientation: The scanned corner orientation is invalid.
errorKind.invalid_corner_orientation_sum: The scanned corner twists do not form a solvable cube.
errorKind.invalid_corner_sticker_order: A scanned corner has an invalid sticker order.
errorKind.invalid_edge_orientation: The scanned edge orientation is invalid.
errorKind.invalid_edge_orientation_sum: The scanned edge flips do not form a solvable cube.
errorKind.invalid_face_count: The cube colors must appear exactly 9 times each.
errorKind.invalid_length: The cube state has the wrong number of stickers.
errorKind.invalid_move_notation: Use standard move notation such as R, U, R' or U2.
errorKind.invalid_permutation_parity: The scanned pieces have impossible permutation parity.
errorKind.invalid_symbol: The cube state contains an invalid color symbol.
errorKind.max_depth_exceeds_limit: Max moves exceeds the API safety limit.
errorKind.max_nodes_exceeds_limit: Max nodes exceeds the API safety limit.
errorKind.missing_corner: The scanned cube is missing a corner piece.
errorKind.missing_edge: The scanned cube is missing an edge piece.
errorKind.node_limit_exceeded: The solver stopped after reaching the configured node cap.
errorKind.notation_too_large: The scramble notation is too large.
errorKind.strategy_puzzle_mismatch: The selected solver strategy does not belong to this puzzle.
errorKind.unknown_corner_stickers: The scanned cube contains an unknown corner color combination.
errorKind.unknown_edge_stickers: The scanned cube contains an unknown edge color combination.
errorKind.unsupported_input_kind: This puzzle input type is not supported by the solve endpoint.
errorKind.unsupported_metric: This move metric is not supported by the solve endpoint.
errorKind.unsupported_puzzle: This puzzle is not supported by the solve endpoint yet.
errorKind.unsupported_strategy: The selected solver strategy is not supported by this API.
errorKind.unverified_solution: The solver returned a solution that failed replay verification.

status.generated_tables_corrupt: Generated two-phase API tables corrupt or incompatible
status.generated_tables_unavailable: Generated two-phase tables unavailable on the API
status.invalid_limits: Solver limits exceed API safety caps
status.not_found_within_limits: No solution within the configured limits

form.scanUnavailableForPuzzle: Camera scan is available for 3x3 only right now.

strategies.meaningDefault: The deterministic backend solver strategy used for this request. It is search logic, not generative AI.
strategies.meaningGeneratedTwoPhase: The strategy used. Generated does not mean generative AI; this is a two-phase solver backed by pre-generated pruning tables. Quality modes try shorter solution depths before falling back to the configured maximum.

instructions.space: Space or touch: hold, release to start, press to stop.

scramble.generateFailed: Could not generate a competition-quality scramble. Try again.
scramble.title: Scramble
